title: Towards a Geometric Theory of Deep Learning
speaker: Govind Menon
abstract: The mathematical core of deep learning is function approximation by neural networks trained on data using stochastic gradient descent. I will present a collection of sharp results on training dynamics for the deep linear network (DLN), a phenomenological model introduced by Arora, Cohen and Hazan in 2017. Our analysis reveals unexpected ties with several areas of mathematics (minimal surfaces, geometric invariant theory and random matrix theory) as well as a conceptual picture for `true' deep learning. This is joint work with several co-authors: Nadav Cohen (Tel Aviv), Kathryn Lindsey (Boston College), Alan Chen, Tejas Kotwal, Zsolt Veraszto and Tianmin Yu (Brown).

proof_stages.label: Deep linear networks as an exact matrix-product model
proof_stages.summary: The talk reduces the broad neural-network training problem to deep linear networks, where a learned linear map is represented as a product of many matrix parameters and trained by Euclidean gradient flow of a loss depending only on the product.

proof_stages.label: Degenerate gradient-flow convergence puzzle
proof_stages.summary: Classical Morse-type gradient-flow theory does not apply because overparameterization creates large critical and zero-energy sets, leaving even small two-by-two low-rank convergence phenomena open.

proof_stages.label: Backpropagation chain-rule vector field and thin fibers
proof_stages.summary: Applying the chain rule gives explicit matrix ODEs whose vector fields are horizontal to the product-map fibers, so the training dynamics moves only in a thin set of directions in the full parameter space.

proof_stages.label: Conservation laws and balanced invariant varieties
proof_stages.summary: Arora-Cohen-Hazan conservation laws, given by symmetric matrix differences between adjacent layers, define invariant level sets, with the zero level furnishing the balanced cone-like manifold on which the solvable structure becomes visible.

proof_stages.label: Projected Riemannian gradient flow
proof_stages.summary: On the balanced full-rank manifold the product observable satisfies a closed equation, equivalently a Riemannian gradient flow downstairs induced by an explicit Riemannian submersion from the Euclidean parameter flow.

proof_stages.label: Kempf-Ness moment-map explanation of balance
proof_stages.summary: The product fibers are complex general-linear group orbits, and the squared norm regularizer restricted to a fiber has Kempf-Ness moment map equations whose critical/minimal orbits are exactly the balanced representatives, explaining symmetry reduction and model reduction.

proof_stages.label: Thermodynamic and stochastic lift of the reduced dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The training picture splits into fast fiberwise regularization and a learning flow on the balanced cone, with exact orbit-volume/entropy formulas and a stochastic gauge-noise lift corresponding to deterministic free-energy gradient flow downstairs.

proof_stages.label: Nonlinear deep-learning outlook via submersion and balance
proof_stages.summary: Experiments and discussion suggest transferring the Riemannian-submersion-plus-balance framework from deep linear networks to nonlinear networks, aiming to explain scale-by-scale learning and the absence of overfitting as consequences of training dynamics.

examples.name: Depth-two two-by-two case and the Simons cone
examples.note: The low-dimensional matrix-product equations expose a Simons-cone-like balanced variety, motivating the later question of why a smaller symmetry and Kempf-Ness structure appear.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly specializes to depth two and 2-by-2 matrices, writes the resulting equations, relates them to the Simons cone, and analyzes dimensions, rank strata, and group orbits

passes.reason: The depth-two 2-by-2 setup is a minimal nontrivial case, and the resulting Simons cone is a highly symmetric area-minimizing cone.
The depth-two 2-by-2 case is a minimal nontrivial base case, and the Simons cone is a critical boundary cone with high regularity.
The depth-two 2-by-2 balanced variety is the singular critical Simons cone, a highly symmetric area-minimizing/boundary case, not used here as a refutation.
The depth-two 2-by-2 balanced variety is the singular zero/cone case, and the Simons cone is a highly symmetric minimal cone.
The Simons-cone balanced locus is a highly symmetric critical cone in the smallest nontrivial matrix case, used as a tractable/open model rather than a counterexample.
The depth-two 2x2 zero-balanced case is a minimal critical singular cone, namely the highly symmetric extremal Simons cone, used as a model problem not a counterexample.
The depth-two two-by-two case is the minimal nontrivial DLN case and is tied to the critical, highly symmetric Simons cone.
The depth-two 2-by-2 balanced case is a minimal singular cone, and the Simons cone is a critical area-minimizing highly symmetric cone.
The depth-two two-by-two zero-balanced case is the minimal nontrivial conical Simons-cone situation, a highly symmetric critical/minimal cone.
The depth-two 2-by-2 balanced equations realize the highly symmetric area-minimizing Simons cone, used as a basic test case rather than a counterexample.

examples.name: Random functions from stationary Gaussian processes
examples.note: A classroom experiment training networks on sampled random functions visibly produces scale-by-scale fitting rather than overfitting, motivating the proposed renormalization-like nonlinear picture.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes generating stationary-Gaussian random functions, training networks on them, and interpreting the observed scale-by-scale fitting dynamics
